I know all the tools say like, hey, you want to match the length of the best article and Google ranks it.

But if we're like saying it long, but like, you know, if you were actually writing this blog post, I would be like very, very militant about like, no, let's share it all over.

I mean, that's a good question.

I think sometimes, like, you'll see articles that end up being 3,000 words long, but because of the way that they do the structure and formatting, like they break up long walls of text with, like, bullet points or, like, different, like, tables or something like that, I think that will make things seem much more readable and approachable than just, like, 3,000 words in paragraph form.
But, yeah, I do, I, it is tricky.
I don't know, I don't know what the right answer is if, Eric or Aida, if you have more insight into this in terms of word count, because that's a good point.
Like, if top ranking pages do have a certain word count, maybe it, like, signals authority or, like, depth of coverage or something.
I don't really know that there is a right answer.
Answer, or right way to approach this.

I mean, yeah, like what you said about fluff, that is something that we definitely want to minimize in the writing, and it's not something that we want to publish if we are going around in circles or just providing information that is just repetitive.
However, like a best SEO practice for a blog post is like probably like 1,200 words minimum.
That's just been our experience so far.
We definitely don't want to do long content for the sake of long content, so we can't be like really like militant about like, okay, we really need this section in this blog post.
But there is like from a, just from an SEO standpoint, like a blog post should be long enough to cover like all of the subtopics so that it fulfills the search.
